---
date: "2026-06-22"
---

## The paper/ Folder and a Complete main.tex

- Part 2 — the finished `paper/` folder and a complete `main.tex`

::: notes
Welcome back to Lab Eight, part two of twelve. In part one we framed the three artifacts and the operational bar for "done." Now we build the first artifact: the compiled paper. We start with the finished paper folder and a minimal-but-complete main-dot-tex skeleton, in the AEA article style empirical economics uses.
:::

## Why a Compiled PDF, AEA Style

- A Markdown draft is for you
- A compiled PDF is for the reader
- The convention: the AEA article style
- Title block, abstract, numbered sections, booktabs tables
- Plain `article` with the right packages compiles anywhere

::: notes
A Markdown draft is for you; a compiled PDF is for the reader. The journals your capstone is modeled on — and the Schar Young Scholars Journal your paper may land in — typeset in LaTeX, and the convention in empirical economics is the AEA article style: a single-column article class with a title block, an abstract, numbered sections, booktabs tables, and a references list. You do not need the official AEA document class to learn the workflow. A plain article class with the right packages produces the same shape and compiles anywhere. So we build the minimal-but-complete skeleton first, and note where the AEA class drops in later.
:::

## The Finished paper/ Folder

- `main.tex` — the manuscript you build now
- `tables/main_results.tex` — generated by code
- `figures/event_study.pdf` — generated by code
- Every table and figure was *written by your code*
- The manuscript reads them; it never contains a coefficient

::: notes
Recall the layout from Lab Seven: your write-up lives in the paper folder, and Week Eight's analysis writes its outputs into paper-slash-tables and paper-slash-figures. By now that folder holds main-dot-tex, a tables folder with main-results-dot-tex, and a figures folder with event-study-dot-pdf — and every file under tables and figures was written by your code in notebooks eight point three and eight point five, never typed. The load-bearing rule of the whole lab lives in that fact: the manuscript reads the table and figure; it never contains a coefficient. The number minus one point eight appears nowhere in main-dot-tex — it lives only in the generated main-results-dot-tex, which main-dot-tex inputs. That is the machine-traversable chain from raw bytes to PDF that Chapter eight point five demands, and it is what makes the honesty test possible.
:::

## The Two Load-Bearing Lines

- `\input{tables/main_results.tex}` pulls in the table
- `\includegraphics{event_study.pdf}` pulls in the figure
- These are the entire reason this is not copy-paste
- A compile trap: do not mix `\cite` with `natbib`
- Manual `thebibliography` plus `natbib` fails the build

::: notes
The skeleton is deliberately small but complete in shape: a title block, an abstract, an introduction, a data section, an empirical-design section, a results section that pulls in the generated table and figure, a conclusion, and a references list. Two things are worth dwelling on. First, the two load-bearing lines are input-tables-slash-main-results-dot-tex and include-graphics of event-study-dot-pdf. They are the entire reason this is not copy-paste. Second, a subtle compile trap that bit the verification of this lab: if you use backslash-cite with a manual the-bibliography block, do not also load the natbib package. Natbib's author-year mode is incompatible with a hand-written bibliography, and the build fails. Use plain backslash-cite with backslash-bibitem, or switch fully to BibTeX with a dot-bib file — but not the half-and-half. The working combination compiles to a clean two-page PDF.
:::

## Dropping In the Real AEA Class

- Only two things change with the real template
- First line: `\documentclass[12pt]{aea}`
- Bibliography: the `aea.bst` style and a `references.bib`
- The body is identical — sections, inputs, graphics
- Learn the shape on `article`; swap when it lands

::: notes
When your camp distributes the AEA template, the only change is the first line and the bibliography mechanism. You write document-class-aea instead of article, you use the aea-dot-b-s-t BibTeX style, and you call backslash-bibliography reading a references-dot-bib file. The body — backslash-maketitle, the abstract, the sections, the input and include-graphics lines — is identical. So learn the shape on the plain article class, and swap the class when the template lands. Nothing else in your manuscript moves.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The manuscript **reads** results, it never **contains** them — `\input{}` the table and `\includegraphics{}` the figure, so no coefficient is ever typed. Learn the shape on plain `article`; the AEA class is a one-line swap. Next: actually compiling it, on Overleaf or locally.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part two: a compiled PDF is for the reader, and the AEA article style is the convention. The whole discipline is in two lines — input the generated table and include-graphics the generated figure — so the manuscript reads results but never contains a coefficient. Watch the compile trap: do not mix a manual bibliography with the natbib package. And the real AEA class is a one-line swap from plain article. In part three we actually compile this paper, two ways: the Overleaf browser workflow that needs no install, and the local latexmk or tectonic build on the container.
:::
